EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass moved into western Massachusetts late Thursday night on January 25th, and remained in place into Friday, January 26th. Early morning low temperatures on Friday ranged between zero and ten degrees below zero. |In addition, northwest winds of 5 to 10 mph produced wind chills as low as 20 degrees below zero early Friday morning, especially across higher elevations.

Early morning low temperatures on Friday ranged between zero and ten degrees below zero.|The coldest temperature recorded in southern Berkshire county was 7 degrees below zero at West Otis.|In addition, northwest winds of 5 to 10 mph produced wind chills as low as 25 degrees below zero early Friday morning, especially across higher elevations.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed east across east central New York and western New England during the afternoon hours of Tuesday June 5th. The interaction of this strong front, combined with a warm, moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Great Barrington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Ohio Valley through the Mass Turnpike region of southern New England, bringing a prolonged period of freezing rain and sleet to portions of north central Massachusetts. This resulted in one quarter to one half inch of icing in parts of northern Worcester and northwest Middlesex Counties, which took down trees, large branches, and power lines. Damage was most severe near the New Hampshire border, including Gardner, Leominster, Ashby, Townsend, and Winchendon. Thousands of electric customers lost power. One woman was injured in Petersham, when she was hit by a falling tree branch. She was pinned to the ground for about 15 minutes, before being freed by paramedics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, large branches, and power lines were reported down in Ashburnham, Gardner, Leominster, and Winchendon. Thousands were left without power. One woman was injured in Petersham, when she was hit by a falling tree branch. She was pinned to the ground for about 15 minutes, before being freed by paramedics.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds in the wake of a polar cold front produced sporadic damage in part of the Connecticut Valley as well as the northeast coast. In Chicopee, sheet metal was blown onto wires, causing a power outage. An amateur radio operator reported a measured wind gust to 56 mph. In Saugus, wind gusts estimated as high as 45 mph brought down wires and one tree on Howard Street. Wires were also downed near Fox Hill Bridge in Revere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheet metal was blown onto wires in Chicopee, causing a power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the southern Plains, into the central Great Lakes on Sunday, December 23rd, while intensifying. The low pressure system then moved into eastern Canada by Monday, December 24th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system swept east across eastern New York and western New England late Sunday afternoon and evening.||Strong to locally damaging winds occurred ahead of, and with the cold frontal passage on Sunday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down in Washington as a result of high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved across northern New England and another low pressure system that formed south of Long Island merged along the southeast coast of Massachusetts.  This storm produced heavy snow across much of eastern Massachusetts.

Cool temperatures aloft spread over Southern New England during the afternoon hours of the 6th. These cool temperatures combined with moderate surface heating to allow for a good amount of instability to develop. Thunderstorms developed in this airmass ahead of a weak cold front and some became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Carolinas strengthened as it tracked over southeast Massachusetts and Cape Cod Bay. This winter storm brought heavy snow and sleet to all but the immediate south coast, where a windswept heavy rain fell and resulted in urban and small stream flooding. Snow eventually changed to a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain in the interior and to all rain near the coast. Snowfall totals of 6 to 14 inches were widely observed.|The region northwest of Boston was especially impacted by the storm. Those portions of Interstates 93 and 495 were littered with spinouts, rollovers, and cars off the road but no serious injuries were reported.|Near the south coast, significant urban flooding closed several roads in New Bedford and Fall River. Small stream flooding was reported in Dartmouth. Wolf Plain Brook in Rehoboth, the Satucket River in East Bridgewater, and Accord Brook in Hingham also rose out of their banks and produced minor flooding of nearby areas. |Official snowfall totals included 16.9 inches in Worcester, 8.5 inches at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, 8.1 inches at Logan International Airport in Boston, and 6.0 inches at the National Weather Service office in Taunton.|Other snowfall totals, as reported by cooperative observers and trained spotters, included 19 inches in Ashfield, 14 inches in Chester, Huntington, Westford, Lowell, and Newburyport, 12 inches in Shelburne, Holyoke, Easthampton, Ashburnham, Leominster, Maynard, and Reading, 10 inches in Sunderland, Amherst, Agawam, Boylston, Shirley, Wakefield, Salem, and Marblehead, 8 inches in Longmeadow, Clinton, Concord, Framingham, and Walpole, and 6 inches in Randolph, Norton, and Brockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals averaged 6 to 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Carolinas strengthened as it tracked over southeast Massachusetts and Cape Cod Bay. This winter storm brought heavy snow and sleet to all but the immediate south coast, where a windswept heavy rain fell and resulted in urban and small stream flooding. Snow eventually changed to a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain in the interior and to all rain near the coast. Snowfall totals of 6 to 14 inches were widely observed.|The region northwest of Boston was especially impacted by the storm. Those portions of Interstates 93 and 495 were littered with spinouts, rollovers, and cars off the road but no serious injuries were reported.|Near the south coast, significant urban flooding closed several roads in New Bedford and Fall River. Small stream flooding was reported in Dartmouth. Wolf Plain Brook in Rehoboth, the Satucket River in East Bridgewater, and Accord Brook in Hingham also rose out of their banks and produced minor flooding of nearby areas. |Official snowfall totals included 16.9 inches in Worcester, 8.5 inches at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, 8.1 inches at Logan International Airport in Boston, and 6.0 inches at the National Weather Service office in Taunton.|Other snowfall totals, as reported by cooperative observers and trained spotters, included 19 inches in Ashfield, 14 inches in Chester, Huntington, Westford, Lowell, and Newburyport, 12 inches in Shelburne, Holyoke, Easthampton, Ashburnham, Leominster, Maynard, and Reading, 10 inches in Sunderland, Amherst, Agawam, Boylston, Shirley, Wakefield, Salem, and Marblehead, 8 inches in Longmeadow, Clinton, Concord, Framingham, and Walpole, and 6 inches in Randolph, Norton, and Brockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 7 inches in Ware to 12 inches in Easthampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong and slow moving coastal storm for mid April tracked to western Long Island Sound on April 16th before weakening slowly and drifting offshore. This storm brought a variety of impacts in southern New England, including heavy snow to the higher elevations of western Massachusetts, damaging winds in excess of 60 mph, widespread river and stream flooding, and significant coastal flooding through several high tide cycles.|Snowfall totals as high as 4 to 8 inches were observed in the higher elevations of Franklin, Hampshire, and Hampden Counties. There were numerous reports of downed trees and power lines in these areas from the weight of the heavy, wet snow. Lesser storm totals of 1 to 3 inches were reported from northern Worcester into northwest Middlesex Counties. Most of this snow fell during the day on April 15th, and changed to rain at night as milder air was drawn into the interior.|East to northeast winds gusted as high as 60 mph in eastern Massachusetts. The highest gusts reported included 72 mph at the Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, 67 mph in East Falmouth, 64 mph on Nantucket, and 63 mph in Plymouth. There were widespread reports of downed trees, large branches, and power lines, as far inland as Taunton, Framingham, and Topsfield. Several roads were blocked by fallen trees in southeast Massachusetts. Wind damage was more sporadic across western and central Massachusetts.|Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches, combined with wet antecedent conditions, resulted in widespread river and stream flooding, as well as significant flooding of urban areas. The worst flooding affected the Merrimack Valley, where moderate to major flooding occurred on the Merrimack, Nashua, and North Nashua Rivers. For many locations, this may have been the worst flooding since the May, 2006 or April, 1987 floods ��� but along the North Nashua, the preliminary crests recorded may have been the highest since the floods of September, 1938.|Many small streams throughout the region also rose out of their banks and flooded nearby areas, including roadways. Major flooding occurred along the Mill River in Northampton, which required the evacuation of nearby residents. |Minor to moderate coastal flooding occurred along the coastline of|Massachusetts through several high tide cycles, due to the combination of strong onshore winds, high seas, and astronomically high tides.|Along the South Coast, the worst coastal flooding occurred with morning high tide on April 16th, where flood waters and debris closed several shore roads. In eastern Massachusetts, the worst coastal flooding occurred with the evening high tide on April 17th. Moderate flooding forced the closure of many shore roads. There were reports of cars stranded in flood waters along the Plymouth County coast. Although no injuries or fatalities were reported, four people including two children needed to be rescued from vehicles ��� one of whom nearly drowned when her car was carried away by flood waters in the Green Harbor section of Marshfield. On Nantucket, one home on Sheep Pond Road fell into the ocean after waves eroded the bluff underneath. Coastal flooding also affected Martha���s Vineyard and Chatham. A breach was opened on Norton Point Beach on Chappaquiddick Island, cutting off access to Edgartown, and also on Nauset Beach in Chatham where several cottages were cut off from the mainland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major flooding occurred along the North Nashua River in Fitchburg. A crest of 8.6 feet was recorded at 530 am on the 16th (flood stage is 6.5 feet). This may have surpassed the previous record high crest which occurred in April, 2007. Widespread flooding was reported along the river in Fitcburg, where several roads were impassable. Minor flooding occurred along the Blackstone River in Northbridge, where the river crested at 10.3 feet at 315 pm on the 16th (flood stage is 9 feet).

Low pressure over the Carolinas strengthened as it tracked over southeast Massachusetts and Cape Cod Bay. This winter storm brought heavy snow and sleet to all but the immediate south coast, where a windswept heavy rain fell and resulted in urban and small stream flooding. Snow eventually changed to a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain in the interior and to all rain near the coast. Snowfall totals of 6 to 14 inches were widely observed.|The region northwest of Boston was especially impacted by the storm. Those portions of Interstates 93 and 495 were littered with spinouts, rollovers, and cars off the road but no serious injuries were reported.|Near the south coast, significant urban flooding closed several roads in New Bedford and Fall River. Small stream flooding was reported in Dartmouth. Wolf Plain Brook in Rehoboth, the Satucket River in East Bridgewater, and Accord Brook in Hingham also rose out of their banks and produced minor flooding of nearby areas. |Official snowfall totals included 16.9 inches in Worcester, 8.5 inches at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, 8.1 inches at Logan International Airport in Boston, and 6.0 inches at the National Weather Service office in Taunton.|Other snowfall totals, as reported by cooperative observers and trained spotters, included 19 inches in Ashfield, 14 inches in Chester, Huntington, Westford, Lowell, and Newburyport, 12 inches in Shelburne, Holyoke, Easthampton, Ashburnham, Leominster, Maynard, and Reading, 10 inches in Sunderland, Amherst, Agawam, Boylston, Shirley, Wakefield, Salem, and Marblehead, 8 inches in Longmeadow, Clinton, Concord, Framingham, and Walpole, and 6 inches in Randolph, Norton, and Brockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 12 inches in Westhampton to 15 inches in Worthington.

Low pressure over the Carolinas strengthened as it tracked over southeast Massachusetts and Cape Cod Bay. This winter storm brought heavy snow and sleet to all but the immediate south coast, where a windswept heavy rain fell and resulted in urban and small stream flooding. Snow eventually changed to a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain in the interior and to all rain near the coast. Snowfall totals of 6 to 14 inches were widely observed.|The region northwest of Boston was especially impacted by the storm. Those portions of Interstates 93 and 495 were littered with spinouts, rollovers, and cars off the road but no serious injuries were reported.|Near the south coast, significant urban flooding closed several roads in New Bedford and Fall River. Small stream flooding was reported in Dartmouth. Wolf Plain Brook in Rehoboth, the Satucket River in East Bridgewater, and Accord Brook in Hingham also rose out of their banks and produced minor flooding of nearby areas. |Official snowfall totals included 16.9 inches in Worcester, 8.5 inches at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, 8.1 inches at Logan International Airport in Boston, and 6.0 inches at the National Weather Service office in Taunton.|Other snowfall totals, as reported by cooperative observers and trained spotters, included 19 inches in Ashfield, 14 inches in Chester, Huntington, Westford, Lowell, and Newburyport, 12 inches in Shelburne, Holyoke, Easthampton, Ashburnham, Leominster, Maynard, and Reading, 10 inches in Sunderland, Amherst, Agawam, Boylston, Shirley, Wakefield, Salem, and Marblehead, 8 inches in Longmeadow, Clinton, Concord, Framingham, and Walpole, and 6 inches in Randolph, Norton, and Brockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches in Randolph to 8 inches in Walpole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 29th. There was enough instability present to allow a storm or two to become severe. The main threat from the storms was torrential downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires down on Stanford Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 29th. There was enough instability present to allow a storm or two to become severe. The main threat from the storms was torrential downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced weak wet microbursts across the Southern portion of Middlesex County on the afternoon on the 29th. Trees were downed in Framingham where a tree split a car in half. Trees were also downed in Sherborn and Ashland which resulted in power outages.

A breach was opened on Norton Point Beach on Chappaquiddick Island, cutting off access to Edgartown, and also on Nauset Beach in Chatham where several cottages were cut off from the mainland.

An unusually strong and slow moving coastal storm for mid April tracked to western Long Island Sound on April 16th before weakening slowly and drifting offshore. This storm brought a variety of impacts in southern New England, including heavy snow to the higher elevations of western Massachusetts, damaging winds in excess of 60 mph, widespread river and stream flooding, and significant coastal flooding through several high tide cycles.|Snowfall totals as high as 4 to 8 inches were observed in the higher elevations of Franklin, Hampshire, and Hampden Counties. There were numerous reports of downed trees and power lines in these areas from the weight of the heavy, wet snow. Lesser storm totals of 1 to 3 inches were reported from northern Worcester into northwest Middlesex Counties. Most of this snow fell during the day on April 15th, and changed to rain at night as milder air was drawn into the interior.|East to northeast winds gusted as high as 60 mph in eastern Massachusetts. The highest gusts reported included 72 mph at the Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, 67 mph in East Falmouth, 64 mph on Nantucket, and 63 mph in Plymouth. There were widespread reports of downed trees, large branches, and power lines, as far inland as Taunton, Framingham, and Topsfield. Several roads were blocked by fallen trees in southeast Massachusetts. Wind damage was more sporadic across western and central Massachusetts.|Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches, combined with wet antecedent conditions, resulted in widespread river and stream flooding, as well as significant flooding of urban areas. The worst flooding affected the Merrimack Valley, where moderate to major flooding occurred on the Merrimack, Nashua, and North Nashua Rivers. For many locations, this may have been the worst flooding since the May, 2006 or April, 1987 floods ��� but along the North Nashua, the preliminary crests recorded may have been the highest since the floods of September, 1938.|Many small streams throughout the region also rose out of their banks and flooded nearby areas, including roadways. Major flooding occurred along the Mill River in Northampton, which required the evacuation of nearby residents. |Minor to moderate coastal flooding occurred along the coastline of|Massachusetts through several high tide cycles, due to the combination of strong onshore winds, high seas, and astronomically high tides.|Along the South Coast, the worst coastal flooding occurred with morning high tide on April 16th, where flood waters and debris closed several shore roads. In eastern Massachusetts, the worst coastal flooding occurred with the evening high tide on April 17th. Moderate flooding forced the closure of many shore roads. There were reports of cars stranded in flood waters along the Plymouth County coast. Although no injuries or fatalities were reported, four people including two children needed to be rescued from vehicles ��� one of whom nearly drowned when her car was carried away by flood waters in the Green Harbor section of Marshfield. On Nantucket, one home on Sheep Pond Road fell into the ocean after waves eroded the bluff underneath. Coastal flooding also affected Martha���s Vineyard and Chatham. A breach was opened on Norton Point Beach on Chappaquiddick Island, cutting off access to Edgartown, and also on Nauset Beach in Chatham where several cottages were cut off from the mainland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cool temperatures aloft spread over Southern New England during the afternoon hours of the 6th. These cool temperatures combined with moderate surface heating to allow for a good amount of instability to develop. Thunderstorms developed in this airmass ahead of a weak cold front and some became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down on Central Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Carolinas strengthened as it tracked over southeast Massachusetts and Cape Cod Bay. This winter storm brought heavy snow and sleet to all but the immediate south coast, where a windswept heavy rain fell and resulted in urban and small stream flooding. Snow eventually changed to a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain in the interior and to all rain near the coast. Snowfall totals of 6 to 14 inches were widely observed.|The region northwest of Boston was especially impacted by the storm. Those portions of Interstates 93 and 495 were littered with spinouts, rollovers, and cars off the road but no serious injuries were reported.|Near the south coast, significant urban flooding closed several roads in New Bedford and Fall River. Small stream flooding was reported in Dartmouth. Wolf Plain Brook in Rehoboth, the Satucket River in East Bridgewater, and Accord Brook in Hingham also rose out of their banks and produced minor flooding of nearby areas. |Official snowfall totals included 16.9 inches in Worcester, 8.5 inches at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, 8.1 inches at Logan International Airport in Boston, and 6.0 inches at the National Weather Service office in Taunton.|Other snowfall totals, as reported by cooperative observers and trained spotters, included 19 inches in Ashfield, 14 inches in Chester, Huntington, Westford, Lowell, and Newburyport, 12 inches in Shelburne, Holyoke, Easthampton, Ashburnham, Leominster, Maynard, and Reading, 10 inches in Sunderland, Amherst, Agawam, Boylston, Shirley, Wakefield, Salem, and Marblehead, 8 inches in Longmeadow, Clinton, Concord, Framingham, and Walpole, and 6 inches in Randolph, Norton, and Brockton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 9 inches in Beverly to 14 inches in Newburyport.

A weak stationary front traversed eastern New York and western New England during Thursday May 31st. This front then gradually lifted to the northeast as a warm front by late Thursday afternoon and evening.||This front, combined with several weak upper level atmospheric disturbances passing through, produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region, some of which became locally severe Thursday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported during a thunderstorm in Otis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Saturday April 14th, and then moved northeast while intensifying, reaching the southern Appalachians by Sunday morning, April 15th, and then just south of western Long Island by Monday morning, April 16th. This low became very intense, with a central barometric pressure falling below 970 millibars upon reaching just south of Long Island Monday morning. The low then headed off the New England coast by Tuesday morning. |As a result of this intense storm, heavy wet snow and sleet fell across portions of the Berkshires from Sunday morning on the 15th through early Monday morning on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred at higher elevations, especially above 1800 feet. |Most snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 5 inches. However, isolated higher amounts in excess of 7 inches occurred across the higher elevations of northern Berkshire County, mainly at elevations above 1800 feet.|The snow eventually mixed with, and changed to rain by Monday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Saturday April 14th, and then moved northeast while intensifying, reaching the southern Appalachians by Sunday morning, April 15th, and then just south of western Long Island by Monday morning, April 16th. This low became very intense, with a central barometric pressure falling below 970 millibars upon reaching just south of Long Island Monday morning. The low then headed off the New England coast by Tuesday morning. |As a result of this intense storm, locally heavy wet snow and sleet fell from early Sunday morning, through early Monday morning before changing to rain. The heavy wet snow and sleet led to numerous traffic accidents and some road closures Sunday afternoon into Sunday night.|Most snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 5 inches, however locally higher amounts of up to 9 inches occurred across the higher elevations, mainly above 1800 feet. At Savoy, 8 inches of snowfall was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Saturday April 14th, and then moved northeast while intensifying, reaching the southern Appalachians by Sunday morning, April 15th, and then just south of western Long Island by Monday morning, April 16th. This low became very intense, with a central barometric pressure falling below 970 millibars upon reaching just south of Long Island Monday morning. The low then headed off the New England coast by Tuesday morning.| |This intense coastal storm spread heavy precipitation across western Massachusetts, starting on Sunday, and persisting into late Monday. Initially, the precipitation fell as a mixture of wet snow, sleet and rain, with snow and sleet more prevalent across the higher elevations. The precipitation then changed to plain rain by Monday morning. Liquid equivalent precipitation total from this storm ranged from 3 to 6 inches. This led to minor flooding across portions of western Massachusetts Monday afternoon into early Tuesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and snowmelt led to minor flooding of small streams and creeks across Berkshire County, beginning Monday morning on the 16th, and ending early Tuesday morning on the 17th.|Minor flooding of low lying, poor drainage areas and roadways led to some road closures. In addition, minor flooding occurred on the West Branch of the Farmington River at New Boston, which crested at 9.23 feet at 08:30 EST on the 16th, and fell back below the flood stage of 8 feet by 18:52 EST on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed along a nearly stationary front across the Ohio Valley on Monday September 10th, and traveled east, moving across southeast New York and southern New England during Tuesday September 11th.||As this low approached, an area of moderate to heavy rainfall occurred, which led to localized flooding in portions of western Massachusetts during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rainfall led to isolated flooding in portions of Berkshire County during the late morning and early afternoon hours of Tuesday September 11th. Flooding closed a portion of Massachusetts Route 2 in the town of Florida from 1143 LST to 1400 LST. In addition, several roads were closed in and around North Adams due to high water, including a portion of River Street, from Houghton Street to Brown Street, as well as a portion of Beaver Street and Mohawk Forest Boulevard.  North Adams City firefighters also pumped water out of several private residences on Pleasant Street and State Road.||

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked from the Great Lakes into southeast Canada on Tuesday morning. A warm front associated with this low moved across eastern New York and western New England early Tuesday morning. With cold air initially in place, a mix of light snow, sleet and freezing rain developed before daybreak Tuesday, before changing to rain by midday Tuesday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet accumulations of 1 to 3 inches fell, which, combined with a coating of ice from freezing rain, created hazardous travel conditions during the Tuesday morning commute. Several weather-related accidents occurred throughout Northern Berkshire county Tuesday morning, according to police reports. One accident, which occurred on Summer Street in Lanesborough, involved a car hitting a tree after skidding on ice, and led to multiple injuries, all of which were not life-threatening.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet accumulations of up to 2 inches fell, which, combined with a coating of ice from freezing rain, created hazardous travel conditions during the Tuesday morning commute. Several weather-related accidents occurred throughout Sorthern Berkshire county Tuesday morning, according to police reports.

A low pressure system developed over the Ohio Valley on Wednesday, December 12th, and moved east northeast, off the northern mid Atlantic coast by late Thursday, December 13th. This spread significant snowfall into eastern New York and western New England, beginning Thursday morning, and persisting into Thursday evening.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 14 inches across Berkshire County, with 13.5 measured at Savoy.||This heavy snowfall led to treacherous travel conditions for the Thursday evening commute, as many roads became impassable. In addition, early dismissals of numerous schools and businesses occurred as a result of the heavy snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system traveled from the lower Ohio Valley on Wednesday, December 12th, to off the northern Mid Atlantic coast by Thursday evening, December 13th. This storm system spread a swath of moderate to heavy snowfall across western New England during Thursday afternoon, before ending Thursday evening.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 9 to 13 inches across northern Berkshire County, with 13.5 measured at Savoy.||This heavy snowfall led to treacherous travel conditions for the Thursday evening commute, as many roads became impassable. In addition, early dismissals of numerous schools and businesses occurred as a result of the heavy snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast along the Atlantic Coast from Sunday, December 30th, into Monday, December 31st, while intensifying. This storm system brought a swath of heavy snow to eastern New York and western New England Sunday night into Monday morning.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 8 to 12 inches across Berkshire County.||The heavy snow led to hazardous driving conditions during the Monday morning commute, and resulted in numerous businesses to close, or delay opening on Monday morning.||Most schools were already closed due to the Holiday break.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed Sunday evening on December 30th, and ended during the mid morning hours of Monday December 31st. Total snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 11 inches, with 11 inches reported in Savoy.||The heavy snow led to hazardous driving conditions during the Monday morning commute. In addition, many businesses were closed, or had delayed openings due to the snowfall.||Most schools were already closed due to the Holiday break.

A low pressure system traveled from the lower Mississippi Valley into the lower Ohio Valley on Saturday, December 15th. This low then redeveloped just off the Delmarva peninsula by Sunday morning, December 16th, and intensified rapidly, becoming the primary low center. This low then tracked into the Canadian maritimes by Monday morning.||Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 15 inches across northern Berkshire County, to 3 to 6 inches across southern Berkshire County, where sleet and freezing rain reduced the amounts. However, a few tenths of an inch of ice accretion also occurred across southern Berkshire County due to the freezing rain.||This significant wintry mix led to the closings of numerous schools and businesses for Monday morning, and also created treacherous driving conditions from Sunday into Monday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet developed early Sunday morning on December 16th, and persisted, heavy at times, into Sunday night. The precipitation then ended early Monday morning on December 17th.||Total snow and sleet amounts of 8 to 15 inches fell, with 15.3 inches reported in Savoy, and 7.6 inches reported at Dalton.||The heavy snow and sleet resulted in numerous school and business closings Monday morning, and also created treacherous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Noel impacted coastal Massachusetts with high winds and coastal flooding.  Trees, branches, and power lines were downed, resulting in an estimated 50,000 customers without power across the South Shore, Cape Cod, Nantucket, and Martha's Vineyard.  Several houses and cars were damaged by falling trees and tree limbs throughout this area as well.  A handful of boats also were beached on Nantucket.  Coastal flooding closed several streets across coastal Massachusetts and the islands of Nantucket and Martha's Vineyard, including the Marblehead Neck Causeway in eastern Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets in Chatham were inundated by coastal waters, including Route 6, which was closed from Chatham to exit 8.  In addition, Route 6A from Route 132 to Barnstable Village was closed due to coastal flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in Mattapoisett and Fairhaven.  A car and a house were damaged by fallen trees.  In addition, the roof of a car wash was blown onto Route 6.  A few power outages resulted from blown down wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across the St. Lawrence Valley locked in cold air across southern New England and combined with low pressure south of the coast to produce a significant snowfall.  Snow fell at rates of up to two inches per hour for an eight to ten hour period resulting in nearly a foot of snow in most locations.  Many motorists were affected as early dismissals from work and school just before snow began created rush hour like conditions which limited the snowplows' ability to plow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a Department of Public Works road salt storage shed collapsed under the weight of ten inches of rain soaked snow.

A low pressure system over the midwest moved east, reaching south central New York State around midday on Monday, January 15th, and then moved off the southern New England coast by Monday evening. Cold low level air remained in place as precipitation overspread the region early Monday morning, producing a mix of freezing rain and some sleet. Significant icing occurred from the freezing rain during Monday, leading to widespread power outages from downed trees and tree limbs, and from power transformers which shorted out. Although the freezing rain tapered off Monday evening, strengthening winds in the wake of the storm continued to down tree limbs and exacerbate power outages across the region into Monday night.|This ice storm had a significant impact on travel and economy across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the midwest moved east, reaching south central New York State around midday on Monday, January 15th, and then moved off the southern New England coast by Monday evening.|Cold low level air remained in place as precipitation overspread the region early Monday morning, producing a mix of freezing rain and some sleet. Significant ice accretion of one half to three quarters of an inch occurred, especially across higher elevations, which led to scattered power outages. Power lines were reported down from the ice accretion in Savoy and Windsor.|The freezing rain ended by late Monday afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure strengthened rapidly as it tracked from the mid Atlantic coast to Cape Cod. The season's first winter storm brought snow, sleet and freezing rain to interior sections of Massachusetts and heavy rain with strong northeast winds along the coast.|Snow began in western Massachusetts during the evening of the 13th and reached the coast by midnight. Milder air coming off the ocean caused the snow to change to sleet and freezing rain during the day on the 14th from the Mass Turnpike region into the Merrimack Valley, and to all rain in greater Boston and points south. Temperatures fell sharply late in the day and during the evening, causing a flash freeze and icy travel conditions. Precipitation changed back to snow before ending around midnight on the 15th.|The highest snowfall totals, on the order of 6 to 12 inches, were observed from the east slopes of the Berskhires to the Route 2 corridor and into the Merrimack Valley. Although snowfall totals only averaged 2 to 5 inches farther to the south, the combination of snow and ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Dozens of minor accidents were reported.|Official snowfall totals included 9.8 inches at Worcester Airport, 2.5 inches at Logan Airport in Boston, 2.1 inches at the National Weather Service office in Taunton, and 1.6 inches at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton.|Some of the higher snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters and cooperative observers, included 13 inches in Ashfield, 12 inches in Greenfield, 11 inches in Goshen, 10 inches in Charlemont and Haverhill, 9 inches in Chester, Huntington, Pepperell, and Townsend, 8 inches in Turners Falls, Lowell, and Georgetown, and 6 inches in Holyoke, West Brookfield, Fitchburg, Hudson, Concord, and Andover.|Strong Northeast winds gusted as high as 50 mph from the Worcester Hills and Merrimack Valley to the South Coast, Cape Cod and the Islands. Nantucket Airport reported a peak sustained wind of 41 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 inches in Framingham to 8 inches in Lowell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly settled south across the region late Saturday, January 13th, before becoming stationary across the northern Mid Atlantic states. A wave of low pressure then traveled northeast along this front, and produced a mix of freezing rain and sleet across the region Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly settled south across the region late Saturday, January 13th, before becoming stationary across the northern Mid Atlantic states. A wave of low pressure then traveled northeast along this front, and produced a mix of freezing rain and sleet across the region Sunday morning.|Ice accretion of up to two tenths of an inch occurred Sunday morning, producing hazardous travel conditions.

This wintry mix led to hazardous driving conditions for the Monday morning commute, and also led to numerous delayed openings, or closings of schools and businesses on Monday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the western Great Lakes on Sunday, December 2nd, moved rapidly east into central New York State by Monday morning on December 3rd, before moving east northeast and intensifying in the Canadian maritimes by Tuesday December 4th. ||As a warm front associated with this storm system approached from the southwest, a swath of snow, sleet and freezing rain developed across much of western Massachusetts Sunday evening, and persisted into early Monday morning before tapering off as scattered rain and snow showers.||Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches across northern Berkshire County, with 1 to 3 inches across southern Berkshire County. In addition, ice accretion of up to several tenths of an inch occurred across southern Berkshire County due to freezing rain.||This storm created hazardous travel conditions for the Monday morning commute across the Berkshires, and led to the closings, or delayed openings of numerous schools and businesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, mixed with sleet and freezing rain at times, developed across northern Berkshire County Sunday evening, and persisted into much of Monday, before tapering off Monday night.||Snow and sleet amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches, with 7.8 inches reported at Savoy, 7.3 at Clarksburg, and 4 inches at North Adams. Much of the snow fell as winds shifted into the west behind the storm system, inducing pockets of heavier snowfall across west facing, higher slopes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi Valley on Tuesday February 13th, and then tracked northeast, reaching the mid Atlantic coast by Wednesday morning on February 14th, while intensifying. The storm continued to strengthen during the day on February 14th, before moving off the New England coast by Wednesday night.|The storm spread a swath of heavy snowfall across western New England, which started during the early morning hours of Wednesday, and tapered off late Wednesday night.| |Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches across the Berkshires, with the highest amounts occurring across northern portions of Berkshire County. Slightly lower amounts occurred across southern Berkshire County, where some sleet mixed in with the snow.|This storm significantly impacted travel and commerce across the region from Wednesday through Thursday, as many schools and businesses were closed. In addition, many roadways became impassable by Wednesday afternoon.|Strong winds also created near blizzard conditions at times during Wednesday afternoon and evening.

Low pressure strengthened rapidly as it tracked from the mid Atlantic coast to Cape Cod. The season's first winter storm brought snow, sleet and freezing rain to interior sections of Massachusetts and heavy rain with strong northeast winds along the coast.|Snow began in western Massachusetts during the evening of the 13th and reached the coast by midnight. Milder air coming off the ocean caused the snow to change to sleet and freezing rain during the day on the 14th from the Mass Turnpike region into the Merrimack Valley, and to all rain in greater Boston and points south. Temperatures fell sharply late in the day and during the evening, causing a flash freeze and icy travel conditions. Precipitation changed back to snow before ending around midnight on the 15th.|The highest snowfall totals, on the order of 6 to 12 inches, were observed from the east slopes of the Berskhires to the Route 2 corridor and into the Merrimack Valley. Although snowfall totals only averaged 2 to 5 inches farther to the south, the combination of snow and ice resulted in hazardous travel conditions. Dozens of minor accidents were reported.|Official snowfall totals included 9.8 inches at Worcester Airport, 2.5 inches at Logan Airport in Boston, 2.1 inches at the National Weather Service office in Taunton, and 1.6 inches at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton.|Some of the higher snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters and cooperative observers, included 13 inches in Ashfield, 12 inches in Greenfield, 11 inches in Goshen, 10 inches in Charlemont and Haverhill, 9 inches in Chester, Huntington, Pepperell, and Townsend, 8 inches in Turners Falls, Lowell, and Georgetown, and 6 inches in Holyoke, West Brookfield, Fitchburg, Hudson, Concord, and Andover.|Strong Northeast winds gusted as high as 50 mph from the Worcester Hills and Merrimack Valley to the South Coast, Cape Cod and the Islands. Nantucket Airport reported a peak sustained wind of 41 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 3 inches in Shrewsbury to 5 inches in Westborough. Sleet and freezing rain also produced light icing with hazardous travel conditions.

Dozens of minor accidents were reported.|Official snowfall totals included 9.8 inches at Worcester Airport, 2.5 inches at Logan Airport in Boston, 2.1 inches at the National Weather Service office in Taunton, and 1.6 inches at Blue Hill Observatory in Milton.|Some of the higher snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters and cooperative observers, included 13 inches in Ashfield, 12 inches in Greenfield, 11 inches in Goshen, 10 inches in Charlemont and Haverhill, 9 inches in Chester, Huntington, Pepperell, and Townsend, 8 inches in Turners Falls, Lowell, and Georgetown, and 6 inches in Holyoke, West Brookfield, Fitchburg, Hudson, Concord, and Andover.|Strong Northeast winds gusted as high as 50 mph from the Worcester Hills and Merrimack Valley to the South Coast, Cape Cod and the Islands. Nantucket Airport reported a peak sustained wind of 41 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 inches in Ware to 5 inches in Granby. Sleet and freezing rain also produced light icing with hazardous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong and slow moving coastal storm for mid April tracked to western Long Island Sound on April 16th before weakening slowly and drifting offshore. This storm brought a variety of impacts in southern New England, including heavy snow to the higher elevations of western Massachusetts, damaging winds in excess of 60 mph, widespread river and stream flooding, and significant coastal flooding through several high tide cycles.|Snowfall totals as high as 4 to 8 inches were observed in the higher elevations of Franklin, Hampshire, and Hampden Counties. There were numerous reports of downed trees and power lines in these areas from the weight of the heavy, wet snow. Lesser storm totals of 1 to 3 inches were reported from northern Worcester into northwest Middlesex Counties. Most of this snow fell during the day on April 15th, and changed to rain at night as milder air was drawn into the interior.|East to northeast winds gusted as high as 60 mph in eastern Massachusetts. The highest gusts reported included 72 mph at the Blue Hill Observatory in Milton, 67 mph in East Falmouth, 64 mph on Nantucket, and 63 mph in Plymouth. There were widespread reports of downed trees, large branches, and power lines, as far inland as Taunton, Framingham, and Topsfield. Several roads were blocked by fallen trees in southeast Massachusetts. Wind damage was more sporadic across western and central Massachusetts.|Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches, combined with wet antecedent conditions, resulted in widespread river and stream flooding, as well as significant flooding of urban areas. The worst flooding affected the Merrimack Valley, where moderate to major flooding occurred on the Merrimack, Nashua, and North Nashua Rivers. For many locations, this may have been the worst flooding since the May, 2006 or April, 1987 floods ��� but along the North Nashua, the preliminary crests recorded may have been the highest since the floods of September, 1938.|Many small streams throughout the region also rose out of their banks and flooded nearby areas, including roadways. Major flooding occurred along the Mill River in Northampton, which required the evacuation of nearby residents. |Minor to moderate coastal flooding occurred along the coastline of|Massachusetts through several high tide cycles, due to the combination of strong onshore winds, high seas, and astronomically high tides.|Along the South Coast, the worst coastal flooding occurred with morning high tide on April 16th, where flood waters and debris closed several shore roads. In eastern Massachusetts, the worst coastal flooding occurred with the evening high tide on April 17th. Moderate flooding forced the closure of many shore roads. There were reports of cars stranded in flood waters along the Plymouth County coast. Although no injuries or fatalities were reported, four people including two children needed to be rescued from vehicles ��� one of whom nearly drowned when her car was carried away by flood waters in the Green Harbor section of Marshfield. On Nantucket, one home on Sheep Pond Road fell into the ocean after waves eroded the bluff underneath. Coastal flooding also affected Martha���s Vineyard and Chatham. A breach was opened on Norton Point Beach on Chappaquiddick Island, cutting off access to Edgartown, and also on Nauset Beach in Chatham where several cottages were cut off from the mainland.

A low pressure system developed over the southeast states during Thursday afternoon on March 15th, and then tracked northeast while intensifying, reaching the Delmarva coast by Friday evening on March 16th, and off the New England coast by Saturday morning on March 17th. |The snow began across the Berkshires during the mid morning hours on Friday, and tapered off early Saturday morning. Some sleet mixed in with the snow during Friday night, especially across southern Berkshire county.|This storm had significant adverse impacts to travel and commerce across the region from Friday afternoon into early Saturday morning. Travel was treacherous for the Friday evening commute, with numerous accidents reported.

Low pressure developed over the mid Mississippi Valley on Thursday morning, March 1st, and then intensified, while reaching the western Great Lakes by Thursday evening. A warm front attached to this low approached eastern New York and western New England from the south late Thursday night, accompanied by a mixture of snow and sleet. Another low pressure system developed along the approaching warm front over eastern Maryland by Friday morning, March 2nd, which then strengthened and tracked northeast, reaching the northern New England coast by Friday evening. |As warm air aloft moved into the region, while cold and dry air lingered near the surface, a significant mixture of sleet and freezing rain occurred over southern Berkshire county. Ice accretions slightly in excess of one half of an inch led to downed tree limbs and powerlines across portions of southern Berkshire county, as well as subsequent power outages. |This storm had an adverse impact on travel across the region during Friday, particularly during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of sleet and freezing rain started during the early morning hours of Friday, and persisted through Friday morning, before changing to plain rain by midday Friday.|Ice accretions of up to one half of an inch led to some downed tree limbs and power lines across the higher elevations of southern Berkshire county, and subsequent scattered power outages.|This storm had an adverse impact on travel and commerce during Friday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low instability and high wind shear environment created a marginally favorable setup for tornades across Central Massachusetts on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado touched down at the intersection of Barr and Pierce Roads in New Braintree. The tornado traveled northeast for approximately 1.5 miles before lifting just east of the State Police Academy on West Brookfield Road. Structural damage was very minor, with only a few windows blown out along with a few twisted fences. The main damage indicator was uprooted trees. Winds were estimated between 65 and 75 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record flooding along the Mill River caused extensive flooding in Northampton. The river crested at 15.6 feet on the 16th, which was the highest crest on record, and well above the previous record crest of 13.7 feet which occurred in October, 2005. Many roads near the river were closed and some evacuations took place as flood waters threatened nearby homes and businesses.|Minor flooding also affected the mainstem Connecticut River through Northampton. A crest of 119 feet was recorded at 430 pm on the 17th (flood stage is 112 feet).

In Haverhill, it crested at 17.6 feet at 230 am on the 18th (flood stage is 15 feet) while in Lawrence, a crest of 29 feet was reported at 9 am on the 18th (flood stage is 20 feet). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the mid Atlantic states strengthened as it tracked over southeast New England. Snow changed to a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain, before an eventual change to rain over most of Massachusetts. Freezing rain persisted in the higher elevations of northern Worcester County, where around one half inch of icing brought down tree limbs and wires in Barre, New Braintree, and Paxton. Several thousand customers were left without power.|Heavy rain fell over eastern Massachusetts, where rainfall totals of 2 to 3 inches were common. This resulted in widespread urban and small stream flooding, especially in Bristol, Plymouth, and Essex Counties. Several roads were closed in New Bedford, Plymouth, Salem, and Peabody because of flooding. Pickerel Brook in Walpole, Buffington Brook in Somerset, and Accord Brook in Hingham all rose out of their banks and flooded nearby areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One half inch of icing was reported in Barre, New Braintree, and Paxton where tree limbs and wires were downed.

The Acushnet River in Acushnet came out of its banks at Hamlin Street.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over Northwesternmost|Massachusetts and strengthened as they moved southeastward during the early evening hours. They eventually clustered together and formed a bow echo. This bow echo produced localized wind damage as it moved through Hampshire, Hampden, and Worcester Counties.

Strong instability, moderate to strong wind shear, and the approach of a cold front set the stage for a widespread thunderstorm outbreak across Massachusetts, Connecticut, and Rhode Island on the 16th. The main result was wind damage to trees and power lines. This was the most widespread severe thunderstorm event of May 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A surging backdoor cold front produced wind gusts estimated|around 50 mph in the towns of Whitman, West Bridgewater, and Halifax. A few trees and wires were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front located over the Great Lakes approached the region from the west late Monday, July 9th. A prefrontal trough well ahead of this front, in conjunction with a lake breeze boundary, and with a hot, humid and unstable air mass in place, led to the rapid development of scattered strong to locally severe thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York State and western New England during the late afternoon and early evening hours of Monday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires were reported down in Pittsfield due to strong thunderstorm winds. Downed trees and wires were reported on Churchill Avenue, Cambridge Street, Onota Street, and Essex Street.||In addition, a large limb was reported down on the corner of Prospect Street and Robbins Avenue. Also, a snapped tree limb downed a primary electrical wire near the Stearns Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot, humid and unstable airmass, interacted with a lake breeze boundary and led to numerous thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during the late afternoon hours of Wednesday June 27th, some of which were severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, power lines and a power pole were reported down due to strong thunderstorm winds in Pittsfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through east central New York and western New England during Thursday afternoon on June 21st, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms, some of which were locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down due to strong thunderstorm winds in Great Barrington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalling warm front across Southern New England was associated with a very warm and unstable airmass. The front was the focus for afternoon and evening thunderstorms...some of which produced wind damage and very heavy rainfall. Large hail was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio wind equipment measured a thunderstorm gust of 74 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid conditions prevailed across Southern New England on the 28th of June. This in combination with an approaching cold front aided in the development of thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Several of the storms became severe due to the rather strong instability in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees downed in Berkeley and Freetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 29th. There was enough instability present to allow a storm or two to become severe. The main threat from the storms was torrential downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several feet of water over route 31 in Westminster. A police cruiser was trapped in the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west, along with a hot, moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of numerous showers and strong thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during the mid to late afternoon hours on Friday August 3rd,  into the evening hours. Some thunderstorms became severe during this time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in North Adams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was accompanied by strong winds aloft on the afternoon of the 15th. Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of this front and quickly became severe across portions of Southern New England. The main culprit was wind damage, aided by the strong mid level winds that were transported to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree downed on Silver Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front was the trigger for thunderstorms on the 2nd of June. The airmass was unstable out ahead of the front. The atmopheric column contained a moderate amount of wind shear which acted to organize the thunderstorms and enhance their severity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs down just east of Route 1. A greenhouse was damaged nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front coupled with a weakly to moderately unstable airmass aided in the development of a few severe storms on the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires down on Robinson Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary coupled with an upper level disturbance allowed thunderstorms, some severe, to develop the night of August 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, combined with the passage of a potent upper atmospheric disturbance, led to the development of scattered showers and embedded thunderstorms during Friday evening on August 17th. Strong winds accompanied these showers and thunderstorms, with some gusts of 50 mph and higher observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two small trees were reported down due to strong thunderstorm winds in Otis. One tree was estimated at approximately 2 feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary boundary was located across southeast New York and southwest New England during Sunday September 9th. A wave of low pressure then developed over the southern Great Lakes, and traveled east along this boundary, leading to the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms during Sunday afternoon across portions of eastern New York and western New England, a few of which became locally severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey Team found straight line wind damage in North Adams.  Most of the damage was confined to a one half mile, by two block area on the campus of the Massachusetts College of Liberal Arts.||Significant roof damage occurred to three college dormitories, mostly from tin flashing that was torn off due to the strong winds. In addition, several trees and large tree limbs were also downed, one of which smashed a car in the parking lot near the alumni office on Montana Street. Approximately 400 students were evacuated due to the damage.||The damage was consistent with maximum wind speeds of near 100 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept across southern New England shortly after midnight. It caused a narrow line of convection, which tapped some strong winds aloft to cause localized damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed by thunderstorm winds on Route 169.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with pockets of moderate instability to help initiate thunderstorms during the late morning and early afternoon hours. A few of the storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail lasted 5 minutes from 400 to 405 pm and covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cool temperatures aloft spread over Southern New England during the afternoon hours of the 6th. These cool temperatures combined with moderate surface heating to allow for a good amount of instability to develop. Thunderstorms developed in this airmass ahead of a weak cold front and some became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front was the trigger for thunderstorms on the 2nd of June. The airmass was unstable out ahead of the front. The atmopheric column contained a moderate amount of wind shear which acted to organize the thunderstorms and enhance their severity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size and penny size hail reported in Georgetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid conditions prevailed across Southern New England on the 28th of June. This in combination with an approaching cold front aided in the development of thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Several of the storms became severe due to the rather strong instability in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires and large branches down on Dighton Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a cold front from the west, along with a warm, moist and unstable air mass in place, led to the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across eastern New York and western New England during the afternoon and evening hours of Saturday, August 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and wires were reported down in Williamstown due to strong thunderstorm winds. Some of the roads that had downed trees in Williamstown included Cold Spring Road, Bee Hill Road, Southworth Street, Green River Road, Belden Road, Oblong Road, John Street, Route 2, Park Street, Northwest Hill Road, and Arnold Street.||A vehicle in a Park Street driveway sustained damage when trees and wires fell from the strong thunderstorm winds.||In addition, a tree fell on Mission Park Drive on the Williams College campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak stationary front traversed eastern New York and western New England during Thursday May 31st. This front then gradually lifted to the northeast as a warm front by late Thursday afternoon and evening.||This front, combined with several weak upper level atmospheric disturbances passing through, produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across the region, some of which became locally severe Thursday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down from thunderstorm winds in Stockbridge. One tree reportedly fell on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary coupled with an upper level disturbance allowed thunderstorms, some severe, to develop the night of August 17th into the early morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree and wires down on Bonnieview Road.
